[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content:
Poke Runyon has done quite useful research on how to scry with black mirrors, and has put out a DVD or two on the subject, which I purchased several decades ago and found quite solid.

These days I can't see anything in scrying mirrors or crystal balls myself, but I occasionally work with one or another scryer who can see (symbolic) things -- sometimes inside them, sometimes on their surface, sometimes only in the scryer's own mind. The trick is to interpret what is seen; it's rarely as straight-forward as geomancy.

There is quite a bit of most helpful older literature on the history and practice of scrying and cxrystal-gazing by solid 19th- and early 20th-century investigators such as Ada Goodrich-Freer, Andrew Lang, Theodore Besterman, Northcote Thomas, and much more recently, Donald Tyson. All of the earlier works are available for download here and there on the internet.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content:
An interesting side story: years ago, when I was involved with the local AMORC lodge in Chicago (which was a really intense and impressive Lodge I have to say) I heard tell of a story that Ralph Lewis, the Imperator at the time, had decided to issue black mirrors to a number of higher degree members to see if they could be incorporated in to the curriculum somehow. Mind you, these were flat panes of glass, not concave as is usual. In any event, the results were... immediate and apparently shocking, because AMORC recalled all the mirrors and shut down the experiment entirely. Apparently people were seeing all kinds of things and a lot of them not at all pleasant. I met a fellow who worked at the Park for awhile, and he told me that all the mirrors were pitched into a dumpster (from which he, being a bit more imaginative and daring, rescued them). Of course, I heard this story and had to know more. My acquaintance, having shuffled off the mortal coil, couldn't tell me anything more about the mirrors, but years later I met another fellow who worked in the park, and, when I asked him, he told me the stuff used to blacken the mirrors was just stuff that was used to dye shoe leather black, not a special concoction at all. W.E. Butler in one of his books recommended using a black enamel paint, again, nothing special, and applying it to the back of a watch glass. So I have made one of these things, and it's in my oratory, has been for over a year now, waiting for me to find time to work on the scrying exercises Butler recommends in his book on now to be a clairvoyant.Hopefully nothing shocking will come up and boil my eyes in their sockets.

Interestingly, the AMORC Neophyte home initiations made use of mirror gazing. THe AMORC student was instructed to set up a home sanctum with a mirror in it, but it was just any old mirror, not a black mirror. Anyway, I met more than a few people who had seen all kinds of weird stuff in the mirror when they did the home initiation.

comments.title: Very Strange Christian
comments.content:
Hi, Kallianeira:

I apologize for not replying to you last week. Didn't quite make it under the temporal limbo wire :D

(Here is that thread (and a fascinating one it is, too):
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/338828.html?thread=56746636#cmt56746636
and the sub-thread with Kallianeira's comment:
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/338828.html?thread=56794252#cmt56794252 )

Well, I never dreamed in 17 billion years that I would ever declare as a Christian. All I'd heard about it was media portrayals, snippets of things mega-preachers said, and what I call the "Christianity Narrative". My parents were not Christian and had no use for it. However, my longstanding friend of 22 years, and former martial arts teacher, one day announced he was doing a Bible study. Intrigued, I poked my nose in. I hadn't even known he was Christian till that day-- after 22 years! He turns out to be a "Bible nerd", and as a nerd myself, that suited me just fine.

By the way, many thanks to commentors here back in 2022 who put me onto the Michael Heiser podcast. I wound up getting Heiser's book "The Unseen Realm", and giving a copy to my new study buddy, who also found it quite intriguing. As you can see, I still read the blogs here and I still collect JMG's books. Ironically, JMG was another influence who started me on this path. He gave advice to a reader in early 2022 or late 2021 to read the Bible starting at Acts 1. For probably-metaphysically-induced reasons, it sounded like a good idea to me, so I started reading it too. Like my martial arts teacher, JMG is a trusted source and I trusted him not to guide us into a tome of toxic sludge.

Between what my study buddy has been showing me since early 2022, an awesome study Bible my stepsister gave me in 2007, and some really helpful comments by the commentariat here, I have realized that I am a creature the likes of which has not been seen in 2,000 years-- an adult Pagan who converted to Christianity. In New Testament times the converted pagans were Greeks and Romans (who have had a massive influence on Christianity as it is practiced today, by the way); I on the other hand started out in Wicca in the '90's, dabbled in some other spiritualities after that, and eventually settled on being a solitary Deist.

What's marvelous is that I have been, and am, engaged in a vigorous conversation with YHWH and Jesus. Very occasionally I have heard what I call "the clear quiet voice". Usually, their* side of the communication is songs on the radio-- Christian pop music stations are enormously helpful for this. I'll have a thought-- not even spoken aloud-- and a song comes on which is an answer. It's way beyond coincidence. A newbie would find it eerie, but I just take it in stride now. Sometimes also there is a dream, or a snippet of dream, or something in the material world will do something perfectly reasonable-looking-- but which you don't usually see happen. I'm not sure if this kind of rich spiritual conversation is common among Christians, or not.

You speak of "the mainstream presentations" of Christianity, and yeah, that's a thing. A lot of Christians (I don't know the percentage though) don't seem to get past what's taught in Sunday school. So I tread softly with other Christians; mostly I don't want to upset them and make them sad.

* Speaking of which, I have learned that 98% of Christians believe in the Triune Godhead, or Trinity, which says that YHWH, Jesus, and the Holy Spirit are all aspects of the same united God. Said Christians can be very, very tetchy about statements which even slightly seem to contradict that. If any such Christians are reading this comment, I apologize for any offense. I have noticed multiple verses where Jesus talks about himself and his Father as if they are separate beings. My study buddy firmly believes that they are not a united Trinity. However, I am still learning and studying-- this and a number of other issues. I will say that when They talk to me and interact with me, there are different "tenors" or "tones" to the communications, which seem like they are from different individuals. Oh, and I got bawled out by an angel once, so that was a third tone. :D

- Cicada Grove

So what you're experiencing has plenty of precedent.

comments.title: Best affirmations are about your identity?
comments.content:
I came across this YouTube video which reminded me of your advice on affirmations:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jWkR-tifY-U

Basically, this guy is a entrepreneur. He said for one year, he did cold plunges in the morning, meditated, visualized and affirmed his goals like a lot of advice out there says. He visualized logging in to see a certain amount of money in his account. He would have annual goals, break them down into quarterly, monthly, daily goals. It didn't work, he didn't achieve his goals.

Then he realized a few things:
- being obsessed with your goals creates attachment to the goals, you reaffirm your existing state of reality
- you should treat your goals like ordering something online. once you set the order, you don't keep refreshing the page.
- focus on your inputs, develop your craft
- if you do affirmations, don't even think about $X dollar amount or whatever, phrase them in terms of what beliefs someone earning that much would have. If you want to earn $10K a month, one belief might be "I'm the kind of person who maintains focus and eliminates distractions", and not just repeat that in the mirror, but live according to that belief

Do you think it's good advice? Is there a place for this kind of specific "I earn X amount", "I do X" kind of affirmations or the identity affirmations are much more useful?

replies.title: Re: Best affirmations are about your identity?
replies.content:
I don't recommend the kind of affirmations that fixate on specific sums or the like, because those are means, not ends. Very few of us want money just to have money (and those who do, need therapy); we want money to accomplish something else. Focus on the goal, not the means! Do you want to live in luxury and have the things you want? Focus on that. Do you want to have the respect of other people? Focus on that. Is there something specific you want to get, for which you'll need lots of money? Focus on that.

Also, he's dead right that an affirmation must be enacted in daily life. If you really do just want to become a millionaire, for example, you might use an affirmation like this:

(on the inbreath) I see and act on opportunities to make money;
(on the outbreath) I use my money to make more money.

Then act on it. Always be watching for ways to make more money, and act on them when you discover them. Always treat every dollar you make as a tool to make more money, rather than spending it.

More generally, though, identity affirmations are potent, probably more potent than any other kind. You can get very far with "I am rich."

replies.title: Not the Original Poster
replies.content:
“… identity affirmations are potent, probably more potent than any other kind. You can get very far with "I am rich."

You astonish me. Based on my interpretation of prior discussions, I would have assumed you’d be sure to dismiss such an affirmation as being too new-agey and demanding of the universe for goodies. I’m really, really sick of poverty, so I will look into it. In case you happen to feel like sharing any further related advice, I will be watching this space closely.

replies.title: Re: Not the Original Poster
replies.content:
Being rich isn't a state of material accumulation. It's a state of consciousness. By this I don't mean some kind of babble about "spiritually rich" or what have you -- I mean actual material wealth. There are two reasons that most people are poor. The first is that they never notice the opportunities for creating wealth that surround them at every moment and press in on them from every side. The second is that they let themselves be conned by others into spending what wealth they do have on things they don't need and usually don't even want. Give up those bad habits and you can be rich.

I'll use myself as an example. I have been very poor -- poor enough that my late wife and I had to choose between buying eggs and buying cheese out of our very scant weekly grocery budget, poor enough that we lived for some time in a cockroach-ridden studio apartment in one of Seattle's bad neighborhoods back when Seattle wasn't yet overpriced. These days I make a very decent income -- about four times what the average freelance writer makes -- and my expenditures amount to about 2/3 of my income. That makes me rich, because it means I can spend money without worrying about it. Do I want to go to Britain to celebrate my 63rd birthday? I can do it. Do I want to drop close to a thousand dollars on the material requirements for a system of magic into which I've just been initiated? I don't even have to check the budget. That's being rich -- not ever having to worry about where the money's coming from.

Mind you, I worked like a dog to achieve that, and I made a lot of mistakes on the way. I found, though, that if I paid attention to opportunities, spent only on what I actually needed or wanted, and went out of my way to avoid the bad advice pressed on me by the shills of the existing order, I could rise out of poverty and become very comfortably well off. You can do the same thing.

Oh, one other tip. You will never be rich working for someone else. Employment is a scam, in which somebody else gets rich by giving you as little and demanding as much as they can get away with. You may need to stay in employment for the moment, but your goal should always be working for yourself, doing something that nobody else can do exactly the way you do. Look for the opportunities -- they exist.

There. Now you know what to do. What you do with that knowledge is up to you.

replies.title: Re: Not the Original Poster
replies.content:
I’m paying close attention.

I also have known years of hardcore poverty, where the family would get into arguments over whether to buy toilet paper or pay the power bill. (The heating got cut once, and I still have a lung affliction to show for it.) No more. ¡Ya basta!

“Mind you, I worked like a dog to achieve that, and I made a lot of mistakes on the way.”

This happened in the case of a college friend of mine, now a successful commercial artist. I have a pretty good idea how hard he had to work to achieve that. We started off in about the same place, but our paths have been different.

“ Employment is a scam…”

I absolutely adore this remark. No more of that “get a real job!” nonsense.

No expectation of a response on your part. You’ve been most generous.

comments.title: Salt
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

There have been several comments about salt deficiencies in the last few weeks. Two days ago, I felt a little light-headed and confused, as I sometimes do, and got the impression that I should eat something. As I'd had breakfast shortly before, I realised that it couldn't be a need for sugar and remembered the salt posts. I ate some salt crystals from my altar bowl and started looking up symptoms of salt deficiency. comments.title: Zarcayce
comments.content: Perhaps you could explain the meme? As far as I understand, the ego is the last thing to dissolve before the cycle of reincarnations on this plane of existence ends. Not to mention (if what I've learned is correct) that the literal translation of Satan in the Bible is ego. Perhaps it's Lucifer trying to get rid of the ego? I don't understand.

replies.title: Re: Zarcayce
replies.content:
Satan translates to "Adversary."

Patrick

replies.title: Re: Zarcayce
replies.content:
Okay, the challenge here is that the word "ego" has many different meanings. Some people use it to represent what we may as well call the spiritual ego, the inner core of personal identity. Others use it to represent the psychological ego, which is simply how the conscious self sees itself. The meme is talking about that latter. It's very common for people these days to have the self-image of someone who's spiritually wise and has therefore transcended the psychological ego, even (or especially) when this is simply a pose of the ego. The no-ego ego trip -- "I'm more egoless than you are!" -- is a pervasive bad habit in North American Buddhist circles, for example.

Btw, "Satan" in the Bible doesn't mean "ego." The Hebrew word הַשָּׂטָן , ha-Satan, literally means "the accuser, the adversary."

replies.title: Re: Zarcayce
replies.content:
If things go right, ego can motivate undertaking practices that have beneficial effects on the structures that the ego is built on top of and depends on, working through systematic back-channel sorts of routes invisible to the ego's centralized threat-recognition and obstruction. The beneficial effects create a kind of distributed resilience, that undermines the ego's ability to centrally compel dysfunctionality (and/or exist, to whatever extent it may be that the existence of the ego means the same thing as the perpetuation of an illusion). But often, ego tries to steer toward sticking with basically-counterfeit practices, that stay within spheres wherein it can continue to stack the deck in interpreting one's actions and their consequences as reflecting well on one's existing self-concepts and existing attachments to imagined futures containing events consistent with one's favorable self-image, so that it can maintain and continue to amass control.

(Though, even counterfeit "controlled opposition" sorts of practices can sometimes produce good effects, if the practitioner is somehow consistently prevented from changing the rules of self-evaluation whenever their implications become inconvenient.)

By way of analogy, think of how a society can identify as being a society of "open scientific inquiry", in a way that motivates it to do things like maintaining open discourse, allowing publication of inconvenient results, and creating institutions for academic freedom. These practices, which result from that society-level ego identity, may, for whatever length of time, push the society towards believing fewer falsehoods and discovering and acting on more practically relevant truths than it otherwise would have.

But usually, a society like that will face a problem of people who are motivated to invoke the formal protections of the norms of open science, but who present or attend to evidence selectively and tendentiously, being motivated by some obvious consistent form of mental derangement or partisanship inconsistent with the underlying spirit behind the explicit norms of science. These people can eventually form their own subcultural identity factions, perhaps even threatening to overturn the saner and more even-handed mainstream. The most convenient thing to do in this case is to solve the problem by rewiring the workings of society's ego a bit, redefining the identity of "scientificness" so that such people are "unscientific".

However, unless there is some consistent source of inner renewal, this just leads to a slippery slope, where more and more subpopulations following the forms of open scientific inquiry are reclassified as "unscientific"; while it starts by cutting out people who are only following the forms outwardly, it goes on to cut out people who are acting from the spirit that the forms were made for. (And that's not even talking about what this does to people working on problems where it was always necessary to have moved beyond those forms. Imagine what might have become of the Allied project of sabotage of the Nazi nuclear program, if straightforward speculative extrapolation of the possibility of nuclear explosives had been deemed to be an "unfalsifiable" "extraordinary claim requiring extraordinary proof", and therefore to be already guaranteed in advance to be false.)

Eventually the society corrupts its conception of "scientificness" so that it stops becoming a matter of verifiable outward forms. "Scientificness" starts being more of an untethered consensus term, that the social ego can redefine increasingly however it wants, against groups that are opposed by whichever form of partisanship happens to be in power at the time. If the targeted group doesn't already have "unscientific" beliefs, it can be caused to have them through a structurally-emergent systemic effect of contrarian polarization. If that doesn't work, the targeted group can still be credibly imagined as being a hotbed for unscientific beliefs, because of imaginary commonalities between its "antinomian defiance" and the defiance of other groups against which the emergent polarization structural "tactic" did work.

In the process, the society's ego gains by degrees the ability to change the rules of evaluation of truth of scientific propositions or success of science-driven policies that it had idealized and identified with, so that flops are silently forgotten, and anyone who tries to make reforms by calling attention to them is invisibly consensus-scootched over into the "defiant" category and devaluated. The ego's "attempt" to get rid of the ego thus, to that degree, gets co-opted into the ego.

replies.title: Re: Zarcayce
replies.content:
Re "satan", here's the wiktionary link.

https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D7%A9%D7%98%D7%9F

The first attested use of the word as anything like a name -- an epithet of office -- is in Job and Zechariah, describing a prosecutor who is perhaps motivated to find excuses to inflict suffering and death, in Job killing the members of a manorial extended household other than the principal owner to try to elicit his supposed iniquity. The word also appears in Numbers 22:22 describing the angel of YHWH sent to block off Balaam's way with deadly force, and in 1 Chronicles 21:1 describing what 2 Samuel 24 instead describes as an angel of YHWH, sent to kill Israelites in a plague for disobedient census-taking.

None of this seems to relate directly to ego; that connection comes later, and then only indirectly three or four steps away, including Zoroastrian cultural influence and the idea of the sin of pride.

That distinction being made, I do admit that, in my limited experience, people who lean into Satanic or Luciferian influences seem to strongly tend to rationalize up galaxy-brained excuses for how they're the only righteous ones and everyone else is contemptibly corrupt. And these accusations, mostly false especially in their connotations and practical implications (though sometimes true in the bare "letter of the law", at least on some reading thereof), are connected to ego, in that the accusations seem motivated by how they serve the ego. This service works by two different pathways. One pathway is that the imagined righteousness confers an imagined license to feel favorably about oneself, especially by comparison to others. (This feels especially motivating if it involves projecting a trait that one is trying to repress shame about.) The other pathway is that the accusations offer a potential path to overturning inconveniently ego-restraining opposed power structures and acquiring ego-consonant supporters.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Is there any esoteric wisdom available for those who have the strong impression that something else has been organising their life for them?
In what is now a long life, there have been a series of strange and unpredictable events, both personal and professional, that have sent me off in different directions. Some have meant small changes, some fundamental ones. In general, these events have come as a complete surprise. The result, both personally and professionally, has been a life which is better described as "interesting" or "varied" rather than "happy" or "successful." I'm mainly conscious of having been used in ways that have been helpful to others.
I was born like everyone with strengths and weaknesses. I was useless at many things, but even as a teenager I could make a roomful of people listen to me and keep them interested and amused. I was instinctively good at meetings and could gently dominate small groups even as a very young man. I somehow had a good instinctive understanding of politics and how organisations function, and could write well and logically without any obvious training. I also had a good singing voice, and at certain times in my life all these things came together in ways that I cannot account for.
But this is not necessarily a life I would have designed for myself. It is not "the life I was born to lead" or any other cliché like that. A lot of it has involved going to places I didn't particularly want to go to, some interesting enough, some boring, some unpleasant or even dangerous. In some cases it was part of the job, but in others I was persuaded or solicited to go. And as the decades passed, everything seemed to build up in a weird fashion, such that things I did or things I could do suddenly became useful and important once again after a long time. And people remembered what I'd done, and would contact me and say "could you..." and I was seldom able to refuse. Just in the last few months I found myself drawing on experiences from nearly forty years ago to help solve some problems, and somehow everything I've done in my life seems to build on everything else in ways I could not have anticipated.
My apologies if this is not very coherent, but in brief I have the strong impression that someone has been very carefully organising my life since I was very small, not necessarily in my immediate interest, and it's not me. I've meditated etc on this but I remain of that view. Is there any esoteric wisdom available?
Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This isn't an unusual experience, all things considered, though it's by no means universal; my life has been shaped by a similar process, for example. In occult philosophy, this is said to mean that you have a great many karmic ties to resolve, and the chance to resolve them. These two things don't always, or often, come together, and when they do, every opportunity is given the soul to take care of its unfinished business, no matter how absurd the coincidences needed to let that happen.

One correction, though -- it is all actually in your own interest. You just can't see that, so long as your perceptions are limited to the perspective of a single incarnation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have had this sense all my adult life. I think of it as my being something like a pawn on a vast multi-dimensional chessboard, being moved here and there by Beings somewhat like chess-masters, who are are playing a game. The rules and aims of this game are wholly beyond my ability to comprehend. Many parts of my life, several unforeseen and hugely compelling sudden shifts in my interests over its course, can only make sense to me on the hypothesis that I have been (and am being used) over the decades of my life to help prepare a small number of other people who in their turn will be pawns in this same vast game long after I am gone.

The details would take far too long to tell, and likely bore readers, too. The briefest summary is that I could not have foreseen even as a 'teen that I would eventually become a professor, an anthropological linguist, a Slavic and medieval philologist, and finally teach courses on the history (and, surreptitiously, on the practice) of magic at an Ivy-league university, and would be a major influence of the subsequent lives of a dozen or two students whom I taught along the way.

Neither could I have foreseen that a month before I graduated from college, a casual friend would introduce me to a fellow student at UC Berkeley, two years younger than me, who (as I was given to know within 24 hours) would suit me better than any other woman I might ever meet in the future. We pursued a courtship, mostly very long-distance by writing letters, for three years, then got married, and have hugely enjoyed our 58 years together so far. (And my premonition turned out to be true. I never have encountered any other woman in all my 83 years of life who would have suited me half as well as she has and still does.)

Nor could I have foreseen the role that my unsought, hours-long visionary experience at age 13 (about which I have written here before) would play in my ability to do right by those dozen or two students. From what I have seen of their subsequent lives after graduation, each of them will make also an important difference, on a small scale, in the lives of those with whom they will interact long after I am gone.

So, the vast and ancient Game continues to be played by those incomprehensible Players. And the very small pawn that I have been will have played to the end its very small part that once mattered a great deal, but will soon be forgotten. And this is as it should be: Ama nesciri et de nihilo reputari "Love not being known and remembered for nothing."

replies.content:
The thing I'm really wondering about is writing: in at least three, and probably six, different parts of the world, 6000 years ago marks the approximate time when the evolution from marking things for various reasons to something resembling writing started. The origins are different (The three that are known to have happened around this time and seem to have no influence from each other are Egyptian hieroglyphs, which emerged from paintings; Chinese writing, which emerged from Oracle Bones, a weird form of marking turtle shells used for divination; and Sumerian from counters for agricultural products)

There is something unnatural about writing and reading; by no means is it inhuman, but it is a very odd thing for us to do with no obvious parallels among hunter-gatherers. There are plenty of possible explanations for why it would have suddenly emerged in multiple places all at once (including archaeologists being arrogant and dismissing older writing!), but it is a very odd thing that it looks like multiple different writing systems all started at once.

If a large number of non-human souls came here, and something resembling writing is natural to them, then it makes sense that some of them would grab whatever they could in order to try to recreate it: thus neatly explaining why writing suddenly appeared in multiple different places from wildly different origins.

I still place a less than 50% chance this vision is true, but it does provide a plausible explanation for something otherwise remarkably strange.

comments.title: Planes, and how they relate, especially the mental
comments.content:
To quickly recap: this is the material plane of matter and energy; the astral plane above this consists of dreams, emotion, and imagination; the mental plane above that is a place of meaning, value, and truth; the spiritual planes after that our beyond our understanding. I gather that souls descend from the spiritual, and rise back up through the planes. I also gather that we have both material and astral bodies, and a mental sheath in varying stages of development; once we have a full mental body, we graduate from being human, and from material incarnation generally.

With that out of the way -- how do these planes relate? Do they mix together? That is to say, are the material and astral planes devoid of inherent meaning and value? Is the meaning down here only what seeps down to us from the mental plane, or from material/astral phenomena pointing upwards? Or rather, can there be material or astral meaning & value? Or maybe there are overlapping cusps, where the etheric side of the material plane overlaps with the astral, and the upper astral has some meaning infused into it from its proximity to the mental -- is that it?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Planes, and how they relate, especially the mental
replies.content:
Not quite.

Material plane: matter and energy.
Etheric plane: life force.
Astral plane: dreams, emotion, and imagination.
Mental plane: meaning, purpose, and value.
Spiritual plane:
Causal plane: (we don't yet know much about these three)
Divine plane

The planes are distinct from each other, but influences can flow from one to another through anything that exists in more than one plane. You, for example, exist in four planes, material, etheric, astral, and mental; you can experience meaning on all the planes because meaning flows through your consciousness from the mental plane.

replies.title: Re: Planes, and how they relate, especially the mental
replies.content:
I see, thanks! So I guess anything/anyone on multiple distinct planes can work as a conduit, including us. I wasn't sure exactly how they interacted, but that makes alot of sense.

Also, I made the mistake of folding the etheric plane into material. There is alot of overlap, but I should give life-force more credit as its own domain (although in my defense, the Western world pointedly ignores life-force, as you've written). In any case, that would explain the Fey or Fair Folk, who appear in our world, yet by all accounts are somewhat insubstantial and bound by different laws -- it's because they exist mainly on the etheric plane, with much less grounding in the material than humans.

In addition, I glossed over the higher planes, which are shrouded in mystery, because we're not ready to comprehend those higher realities yet. I get the impression that the Causal plane is a sort of intermediary between the Divine source and the Spiritual plane, with Divine acts percolating down to us via the latter, but that's pure guesswork on my part.

comments.title: Kilner lenses and intrinsically photosensitive ganglion cells
comments.content:
Something important slipped my mind in the discussion of color perception last week.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intrinsically_photosensitive_retinal_ganglion_cell#Possible_role_in_conscious_sight

Kilner's protocol might be a way to train some of these cells to participate in (fuzzy low-resolution) visual perception, the same way you can treat amblyopia (lazy eye) by forcing yourself to perform visual tasks with an eyepatch over the dominant eye that was covering for the lazy one. In that case, it might take quite some time, work better in younger people, and not work at all for some people. (Fully blocking light to ordinary rods and cones might also not work: you might have to give those cells just enough of an image for the neurons downstream of both kinds of detectors to give reports back to the photosensitive ganglia that they should be gaining confidence that they're onto something important, while still making sure not to give them so much of an image that the photosensitive ganglia aren't "priced out" of having any influence because the rods and cones had already done all the work.)

I don't know what to make of the possibility that etheric para-light, or whatever, would be better at causally interacting with proper ganglions than with rod or cone cells. (Or para-causally, e.g. inserting the equivalent of a writer's note to themselves reminding themselves to make a perception of an etheric body come up "by chance action of neural noise" in some representation corresponding to that part of the visual field, twenty layers of neural processing later.) In any case, though, the photosensitive ganglion hypothesis would lead to a question of confounding from near-ultraviolet ordinary photons, and from the downstream cognition required to interpret them (given the much lower resolution).

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Q1 I'm sure that you've met people involved in many types of esoteric practice throughout your life. Have you met/do you know any people who exclusively use New Thought methods? If so, are these people successful with New Thought? It seems like a lot of corporate/business people are fond of it.


Q2 In contemporary Druidry, is there a specific end-goal that people work towards? Like enlightenment, perhaps? I'm trying to figure out if the goal is purely subjective/personal or if everyone is broadly moving towards the same thing.


Q3 In the Saint-Martin stream of Martinism are there any methods for changing circumstances, or is it purely mystical in nature?


Many thanks as always.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes. Some are very successful and some are disastrously unsuccessful. I've commented elsewhere that there are two broad traditions within New Thought, which might as well be called the pragmatic and the psychotic currents; the pragmatic current recognizes that New Thought methods have to be combined with common sense and hard work, while the psychotic current insists that New Thought methods alone will make material reality obey your every whim. I'll let you guess which current leads to success.

2) In contemporary Druidry, no two people are doing the same thing! It's an extraordinarily diverse tradition; "ask three Druids, get five answers" is a common and accurate joke.

3) The lineages I'm familiar with focus on prayer and meditation, and teach that if you get your inner life straightened out, your material circumstances will sort themselves out promptly. That does seem to work tolerably well, for what it's worth.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi (OP here),


Thanks for answering.


Q1. When you say sorting out your inner life, what does that mean in the Martinist tradition (Saint-Martin current)?


Many thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In Martinist teaching -- and of course in most people's experience -- our inner life is a jumbled mess of conflicting cravings, stray thoughts, unruly passions, and the like. Through prayer and meditation, that mess can be sorted out into something calmer, clearer, and more coherent; as that happens, one's outer life improves.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
If I understand occult philosophy correctly, there are whole ecosystems of beings in the Unseen, and these are most often experienced as something in one’s consciousness. So if thoughts, emotions, ideas and so on are types of these beings, where is the line between ‘me’ and all these Unseen ecosystems? Am I just the awareness that experiences these things? What about my memories? I’m wondering about psychology in light of this; for example, could people blame an issue on evil spirits or demons that actually might be a psychological issue, and vice versa?

I recall that Dion Fortune said to first eliminate all psychological possibilities before investigating whether spirits are involved, but I'm curious how one can tell the difference.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The real "you" is a center of pure being, consciousness, and will. Everything else you think of as "you" is a vesture or body composed of the substance of one of the four planes of being -- mental, astral, etheric, and material -- and is affected by other phenomena and beings of its corresponding plane. Thus your material body, for example, isn't "you" -- it's a structure that makes it easier for you to experience and affect the material plane, and it's constantly being affected by other material things, such as heat, cold, air, water, things you trip over, annoying billboards, and the like.

2) Remember that she received formal training as a Freudian psychologist, and she was speaking to other people who had at least some training of that kind. (It was quite readily available, before psychotherapy professionalized itself into a tightly restricted club.) There's a range of tests that used to be used back in the day to identify neuroses and the like, and this is what Fortune has in mind.

replies.content:
Thank you for your answer!

If magic path is the active use of will and imagination to cause change in consciousness, and occult path is a kind of passive use of those faculties (an analogy I've encountered describes meditation as a state of a fisherman who throws the bait and waits for the fish to bite at it), what is mystical path? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have yet to encounter a really good introduction to mysticism for the modern beginner. The closest I've yet found is Evelyn Underhill's Practical Mysticism:

https://www.globalgreyebooks.com/practical-mysticism-ebook.html

Magic focuses on power, and uses ritual to cause appropriate change in the cosmos; occultism focuses on wisdom, and uses meditation to perceive what the cosmos is doing and move in harmony with it; mysticism focuses on love, and uses prayer to become one with the cosmos and its Source.

comments.content:
For protection against slanderous and false accusations, which planet is best?

For permanent protection from evil spirits that are attracted to our ceremonies when we do them, which protection is best?

For scrying in spirit vision, which planet or power best aids the practice besides our daily practice? Modern lodges say the Moon.

For improving our divination skills besides daily study and practice, which planet is best? This is tricky as there are many planets across many sources that go beyond Mercury and Moon. Saturn and Sun are also recommended. The procedure though is never revealed.

For connecting to the ruling spirit of our neighbourhood or city, how is this done? Do we scry to a publicly available image of our city and find the spirit there or do you think discussing this with a spirit we already have a mutual relationship with is better for this?

- Yarmulk

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
All of this assumes that calling on power outside the self is the best way to do any of these things, and in my experience, that's not even remotely true. Let's take them one at a time.

1) Slanderous and false accusations? JK Rowling is one of the best mages in the world when it comes to this. Have you noticed that she's gone out of her way to annoy extremists on both ends of the political spectrum -- and has profited mightily by it? She doesn't use planetary magic for that. She states her views, and then just lets her opponents make themselves look ridiculous. I've learned a lot from her -- and while I don't make anything like as much money as she does, it's still the case that every round of slander I get from either side is followed by an uptick in my writing income.

2) Protection from evil spirits? That's the easiest thing in the world. There's a scene in Marlowe's play Doctor Faustus where Faustus asks Mephistopheles why he can leave Hell to come to earth, and the demon answers, "Why, this is Hell, nor am I ever out of it." Marlowe knew the secret of demonolatry, which is that you can only summon demons by descending to their level. In the same way, the best way to banish them is to rise above their level.

3) Scrying in the spirit vision? No planet will benefit you anything like as much as plain ordinary daily practice.

4) Improving divination skills? Same answer. You have to do the work yourself; the planets won't help you cheat.

5) Connecting to the ruling spirit of your neighborhood or city? Meditation, combined with a lively involvement in local affairs, will do that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
So Faust could have gotten free from his bargain by training himself to stop calling on Mephistopholes for every little thing, and maybe giving away his ill-gained resources? He didn't really need to turn to Christ?

Or is there a distinction between inadvertently attracting a demon, and entering into a contract with one? (The play strongly implies that the contract signed in blood was only to trick Faust into thinking he is in too deep to repent, and had no real binding power on him.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, he would have had to do something to raise his level of consciousness above the Hell level, and as a baptized Christian in a Christian society, the easiest and probably the best way for him to do that would have been to turn to Christ.

2) There's a major difference, and it has to do with the orientation of the will. Entering into a contract means aligning your will with the demon's, and thus furthering the agenda of Hell. Signing the contract in blood is meaningless, except as a psychological trick, but willingly agreeing to do so is a very serious thing. It's not irrevocable, but breaking the contract and rising up out of Hell is a very difficult thing:

“The gates of hell are open night and day;
Smooth the descent, and easy is the way:
But to return, and view the cheerful skies,
In this the task and mighty labor lies.”

comments.title: On contemplating too much of ____
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

In the same vein as imitating what one contemplates, does an above-average amount of worry regarding a particular thing (e.g. crime, accidents, disease) affect a higher plane (such as the astral) to the point that the particular thing will show up in the material plane in one's life?

As an overly anxious person, I know that I obsess over such things, but my intent is to brainstorm avoiding or preventing. But does the simple (f)act that I'm contemplating such things only gives it even more energy, and therefore it becomes more prevalent?

If so, I shudder at all the astral pollution that can be directly traced back to TV shows and movies.

-Eugene

replies.title: Re: On contemplating too much of ____
replies.content:
If you contemplate a negative turn of events, you must always follow it promptly with a well-considered and clearly imagined contemplation of how you will deal with it successfully. That way, you build an egregor of success despite adversity.

I'm also prone to anxiety in some ways, and one consequence is that I have a plan B for just about everything. No matter what the crisis, I know what I'll do in response. That turned out to be enormously helpful when my late wife's health finally collapsed and I had to take care of her through her dying process. Was it an absolute gut-punch? Of course. Was I miserable and terrified? Sure. That didn't matter. I had a long list of things to do, while she was dying and then after her death, but I knew what they were, and I did them. That allowed Sara to die peacefully at home in my company, as she wished, rather than having to die alone in a hospice or hospital, which was one of her greatest fears. It also got me through the process in one piece, which was no small feat. Once it was all over, I could spend a while sobbing hysterically into my pillow, as I did so very often as a small child, and then pick myself up and start rebuilding my life.
